Re:
Farn PolrrrcAI- PRACTTcES CorrarrarssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 18,2005
Jeffrey A. Walter, City Attomey
City of Martinez
c/oWalter & Pistole
670 West Napa Street, Suite F
Sonoma, CA 95476
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-05-021
Dear Mr. Walter:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Martinez City Council
member Mark Ross regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the'Act').l Since your request is of a general rrutor", we are treating it as a request
for informal assistance.' Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaiuate any
conduct which has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as
the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re ogtesby (1975) 1 FppC ops. 71.)
QUESTIONS
Does Councilmember Ross have a potential conflict of interest under the Act that
would prohibit him from participating in a govemmental decision involving the specific
plan where:
(a). The business from which he receives a source of income manages properties
that are located in the specific plan area;
(b). He has received commissions from this source of income for obtaining
tenants for leases on properties located within the specific plan area;
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2 Informal assistance does not "o.rf". 
the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 2
(c). He is a licensed real estate broker and has obtained a listing, as a real estate
agent and independent contractor, for another one of his sources of income, on property
located within the specific plan area;
(d). Each of these sources of income have a leasehold interest in the proposed
specific plan area;
(e). He secures a purchaser for the property in (c), above, for which he would be
entitled to receive a commission in excess of $500, and the escrow opens, but does not
close, or if the escrow opens and does close, prior to the date of the final decision on the
proposed specific plarfl
CONCLUSION
(a).-(d). Councilmember Ross does not have a conflict of interest prohibiting him
from participating in the governmental decision unless the financial effects of the
govemmental decision are substantially likely to cause amateial financial effect on
either of Councilmember Ross's source of income economic interests in (a) through (d)
above, and as further discussed below.
(e). Once Councilmember Ross has secured a purchaser, and an escrow has
opened, he has an economic interest in any person he represents in the transaction, as the
source of commission income' he is entitled to receive, as well as the business entity
under whose auspices he works.
FACTS
The City of Martinez is considering the adoption of a specific plan for the city's
downtown (the "Specific Plan").
Councilmember Ross is employed by Ross Company Realty Property
Management ("RCRPM'), which is a property management company. The company
does business in Contra Costa County, including the City of Martinez. RCRPM is wholly
owned by Councilmember Ross's parents as a sole proprietorship. Councilmember Ross
has no ownership interest or investment interest in the company. Councilmember Ross
performs property management services for those properties assigned to him by RCRPM.
He is paid an agreed upon salary for the management work he performs for RCRPM.
Councilmember Ross is not paid for his management services from any particular stream
of income derived from the properties he manages for RCRPM. Some of the properties
managed by RCRPM are within the proposed specific plan area. Councilmember Ross
provides property management services for one or more of those properties located in the
proposed specific plan area.
From time to time, Councilmember Ross is paid a commission by RCRPM for
facilitating lease agreements with new tenants for the properties managed by RCRPM.
3 Assuming the income is $500 or more.


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 3
For securing new tenants, Councilmember Ross's commission is based on a fixed
formula, which represents a particular percentage of the first month's rent.
Councilmember Ross receives the commission directly from RCRPM. He does not
receive any monies from the tenants he has secured. During the past twelve months,
Councilmernber Ross has received trvo to four commissions for new lease agreements for
properties located in the proposed specific plan area. None of the commissions
Councilmember Ross has received have individually exceeded $250.00.
Councilmember Ross's parents also own areal estate company called Ross
Company Realtors (RCR). This real estate company is also a sole proprietorship wholly
owned by Councilmember f;.oss's parents. Councilmember Ross holds no ownership in
this company and has no investrnent interest in the company. Although Councilmember
Ross has his own real estate broker's license, he is not the designated broker or the broker
in charge of RCR. Councilmember Ross acts as an independent contractor for RCR. He
is paid a designated percentage of the commission for the sale and purchase of real
property which result from his efforts. Councilmember Ross receives this commission
directly from RCR. He receives no direct payments from either the purchaser or the
owner of the property at issue. At present, RCR has one listing for a property located in
the proposed specific plan area. Councilmember Ross obtained this listing and, should
the subject property be sold, he will be paid a commission from RCR.
Both RCRPM and RCR lease an office in a building located in the proposed
specific plan area.
ANALYSIS
PotrNtlal Coxrr,rcr or INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-intereSt provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making,participattng in making, or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
Srrps I &2: Is CoT.INCTLMEMBERRoSS A Puslrc Orrrcrnr.MancNc, Panrrcrpnrnvc
IN MAKING, OR INFLUENCTNG A GOVTnXnTENTAL DTCTSTOX?
As a member of the Martinez City Council, Councilmember Ross is a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 4
reasonably foreseeable mateial financial effect on any of his economic interests.
Councilmember Ross will be called upon to consider whether the City should approve or
disapprove the adoption of the Specific Plan. Therefore, he will be making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a govemmental decision.
srEP 3: Dors cotxcrr,vrEMBER Ross rravn a pornNrrar,r,v Drsqu.rr.rF-rrNc
EcoNoprrc Ixrnnnsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (g 87103(a); reg. 78703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
($ 871 03(d) ; reg. 187 03.1 O));
a
o
a
a
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more ($ S7103(b); reg. 18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(c); reg.
t8703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him orher if the gifts aggregateto $360
ormore within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(e); reg. 18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects,, rule
($ 87103; reg. 18703.5).
Under the facts you have presented, Councilmember Ross does not have an
economic interest under section 87103 (a), (b), (d), or (e). Additionally, you have not
provided any facts to indicate that Councilmember Ross has an economic interest in any
source of gifts to him. Accordingly, the only economic interest you have asked us to
address is his source of income derived from the two businesses owned by his parents.
Source of Income
Regulation 1 8703.3(a)(1) provides
"For purposes of disqualification under Government code sections
87100 and 87103, apublic official has an economic interest in anyperson
from whom he or she has received income, including commission income


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 5
and incentive compensation as defined in this regulation, aggregatingfive
hundred dollars ($500) within 12 months prior to the time when the
relevant governmental decision is made. A public official's income
includes income which has been promised to the public official but not yet
received by him or her, if he or she has alegally enforceable right to the
promised income."
Regulation 1 8703.3 (c)( l ) provides that "' [c]ommission income' means gross
payments received by a public official as a result of services rendered as a broker, agent,
or other salesperson for a specific sale or similar transaction. Commission income is
received when it is paid or credited."
In a subsequent telephone conversatron, you clarifiedthat, although
Councilmember Ross is a licensed real estate broker, any commission income he receives
from his source of income as set forth in your facts will be for his role as areal estate
sales agent or other salesperson.
Regulation 18703.3(c)Q)(C) provides that sources of commission income in a
specific sale or similar transaction include for a real estate agent:
"(i) The broker and broker business entity under whose auspices
the agent works;
(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and
(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for
referring aparty to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker."
Regulation 18703.3(c)(2XDXiii) provides that sources of commission income in a
specific sale or similar transaction for a salesperson include the "business entity for
which the agent or salesperson is arr agent."
Under the facts you have presented, assuming Councilmember Ross receives
income aggregating $500 or more in the twelve-month period prior to the time the
govefirmental decision is made, Councilmernber Ross has a source of income economic
interest in his employer RCRPM for his salary and the commission income derived from
the leases he facilitates. As long as Councilmember Ross does not represent any other
person in the lease transaction, other than RCRPM, he would have no other source of
income interest in the commission he receives for facilitating lease agreements for
RCRPM as described in your facts.
Additionally, Councilmember Ross has an economic interest in RCR, assuming
he receives more than $500 in the twelve-month period prior to the governmental
decision, for commission income he receives as a sales agent for the sale and purchase of


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 6
real property. Councilmember Ross will also have an economic interest in anyperson he
represents in the transaction; again, assuming he receives a commission of $500 or more.
You have also indicated that Councilmember Ross has his own real estate
broker's license. If Councilmember Ross receives income of $500 from any source for
sale of real property, uqder his real estate broker's license, he would also have an
economic interest in the source of that income. (See regulation 18703.3(c)(Z)(B).)a },ou
have not given us any facts with respect to other potential sources of income economic
interests.
Property in Escrow: We have consistently advised that merely having a listing on
a property does not constitute promised income. (Randolph Advice Letter, No. I-02-
304.) However, once Councilmember Ross secures a purchaser and"asale is pending on
the listed property, the client is deemed to be a source ofpromised income. Once escrow
is opened, there is a buyer and a seller, and the element of speculation has diminished."
(Randolph, supra; Hahn Advice Letter, No. 4-99-239.) "Therefore, those who have
entered into an escrow agreement or acfually paid commission income during the past
twelve months are considered to be sources of income." (Randolph, supra; Wittier
Advice Letter, No. 4-00-164.)
Once the escrow has closed, and the title has been transferred, Councilmember
Ross would still have an economic interest in his source of income for the commission
received on the sale of the property. Whether or not this would present a conflict of
interest for Councilmernber Ross in participating in a governmental decision depends on
the degree of involvement and the corresponding material affects of the governmental
decision on that economic interest.
Since you have not provided information with respect to other potential economic
interests, such as the clients Councilmember Ross represents in any real estate
transactions,for purposes of this analysis we consider only Councilmember Ross's
economic interest tn RCRPM and RCR-
Srpp 4: Is Tm Ecowonznc Ixrrnpsr Drnncrr,y oR INDTRECTLy Ixvor,vro rN TEE
GovnnNyrENTAL Drcrsron ?
In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions that affect
a This would include any person he represents, any person represented by an agent working under
his brokerage license, any brokerage entity through which he conducts his business, and any person to
whom he-pays a finders fee for referring a party to the transaction to him.
5 Councilmember Ross is urged to consider other possible sources of income that may give rise to
an economic interest prohibiting him from participating in the governmental decision. For example, if he
secures a buyer for the property he has listed within the Specific Plan area, this may give rise to a conflict
of interest prohibiting him from participating in the govemmental decision as a result of the economic
interest he has in that source of income.


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 7
sonrces of income, the standards set forth in regulation 18704.1 aWly. (Regulation
rc70a@)Q).)
Regulation 187 }a.l @) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agerLcy when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeaT, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's ageflcy.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
Under the facts you have presented, it appears that both RCRPM and RCR are
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision.
Srrp 5: MlrrnraurySrANDARD
Source of fncome: Once the degree of involvement is determined, Step 5 of the
conflict of interest analysis addresses the applicable materiality standard. Regulation
18705.3(b) sets forth the materiality standards for economic interests in a source of income
that are indirectly involved in the governmdntal decision. Under regulation 18705.3(bxl),
for sources of income that are business entities, the materiality standards set forth in
regulation 18705.1(c) apply. Underregulation 13705.1(c), the applicablemateriality
standards are determined by the size of the business. For example, under regulation
18705.1(c)(4), the financial effect of a governmental decision on a relatively small business
that is indirectly involved in the govemmental decision is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decjsion will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business antity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 8
(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Srnp 6: RrasoNasr.y Fonnsrnanr,p
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulationl8706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certainto be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Regulation 18706(b) and (c) provide:
"(b) In determining whether a governmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an economic
interest as defined in subdivision (a) above, the following factors
should be considered. These factors are not intended to be an
exclusive list of the relevant facts that may be considered in
determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable,
but are included as general guidelines:
"(1) The extent to which the official or the official,s source
of income has engaged, is engaged, orplans on engaging in
business activity in the jurisdiction;
"Q) The market share held by the official or the official,s
source of income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official,s source
of income has competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the govemmental decision in question;
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material
financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not
including future govenrmental decisions by the official,s'agency,
or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the official's agency.
and


File No. I-O5-021
Page No. 9
"(c) Possession ofa real estate sales or brokerage license,
or any other professional license, without regard to the official,s
business activity or likely business activity, does not in itself make
a material frtancial effect on the official's economic interests
reasonably foreseeable. "
The question is essentially whether the approval of the Specific Plan will so
significantly increase business opportunities for RCRPM and RCR that the increased
opportunities constitute a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect. (Olson Advice
Letter, No. 4-00-237 copy enclosed; Bardos Advice Letter,No. A-99-l 99; rhorner,
supra.)
For example, in the Teasley Advice Letter, No. I-97-545,we advised:
"Your questions indicate that you and other agents of
Coldwell Banker Town & County intend to take advxiage of the
business opportunities that will arise as a result of the development
at Hidden Creek Ranch (e.g., becoming a listing orbuyers, agent
when homes or commercial development built under the specific
plan are put on the resale market; becoming a listing agent for
home owners who want to sell their existing homes in order to
purchase new homes built under the specific plan; becoming a
referring source for resale listings for one or more merchant
builders; opening a New Home Sales Division; selling parcels
owned by the seven separate owners). Therefore, it is reasonably
foreseeable that approval of the specific plan will have a financial
effect upon you and Coldwell Banker Town & Country, a
potentially disqualifying source of income to you....,,
However, you have not provided any facts relative to the specific governmental
decision in question that would enable us to evaluate its potential financial effects on
Councilmember Ross's economic interest as outlined above. (See Olson, supra and
Teasley, supra.) Unless the financial effects of the govemmental decision are
substantially likely to cause a material financial effect under regulation 18705.1(c) on one
of Councilmember Ross's economic interests, he would not have a conflict of interest in
participating in the decision. The mere fact that RCRPM and RCR either manage or list
for sale, and lease property within the specific plan area is not sufficient to establish the
reasonably foreseeability of a material financial effect on Councilmember Ross's
economic interests as a result of the governmental decision.


File No. I-05-021
Page No. 10
srnps 7 ANo 8: Purr,rc GrNnnar.lv & Lrcnr,r,v Rrqurnrn p.tRrrcrpATroN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally', or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable herein.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By:
Lenkeit
Division
WJL:rd

tenants for leases on properties located within the specific plan area;

The City of Martinez is considering the adoption of a specific plan for the city's
downtown (the "Specific Plan").
Councilmember Ross is employed by Ross Company Realty Property
Management ("RCRPM'), which is a property management company. The company

He is paid an agreed upon salary for the management work he performs for RCRPM.
Councilmember Ross is not paid for his management services from any particular stream
of income derived from the properties he manages for RCRPM. Some of the properties
managed by RCRPM are within the proposed specific plan area. Councilmember Ross
provides property management services for one or more of those properties located in the
proposed specific plan area.
From time to time, Councilmember Ross is paid a commission by RCRPM for
facilitating lease agreements with new tenants for the properties managed by RCRPM.
3 Assuming the income is $500 or more.
For securing new tenants, Councilmember Ross's commission is based on a fixed
formula, which represents a particular percentage of the first month's rent.
Councilmember Ross receives the commission directly from RCRPM. He does not
receive any monies from the tenants he has secured. During the past twelve months,
Councilmernber Ross has received trvo to four commissions for new lease agreements for
properties located in the proposed specific plan area. None of the commissions
Councilmember Ross has received have individually exceeded $250.00.
Councilmember Ross's parents also own areal estate company called Ross
Company Realtors (RCR). This real estate company is also a sole proprietorship wholly

Both RCRPM and RCR lease an office in a building located in the proposed

sections.analysis:
PotrNtlal Coxrr,rcr or INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-intereSt provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making,participattng in making, or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.

IN MAKING, OR INFLUENCTNG A GOVTnXnTENTAL DTCTSTOX?
As a member of the Martinez City Council, Councilmember Ross is a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable mateial financial effect on any of his economic interests.
Councilmember Ross will be called upon to consider whether the City should approve or
disapprove the adoption of the Specific Plan. Therefore, he will be making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a govemmental decision.

EcoNoprrc Ixrnnnsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial

member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic

An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (g 87103(a); reg. 78703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
($ 871 03(d) ; reg. 187 03.1 O));

a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more ($ S7103(b); reg. 18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(c); reg.
t8703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him orher if the gifts aggregateto $360
ormore within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(e); reg. 18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects,, rule
($ 87103; reg. 18703.5).
Under the facts you have presented, Councilmember Ross does not have an
economic interest under section 87103 (a), (b), (d), or (e). Additionally, you have not
provided any facts to indicate that Councilmember Ross has an economic interest in any
source of gifts to him. Accordingly, the only economic interest you have asked us to
address is his source of income derived from the two businesses owned by his parents.

Regulation 1 8703.3(a)(1) provides
"For purposes of disqualification under Government code sections
87100 and 87103, apublic official has an economic interest in anyperson
from whom he or she has received income, including commission income
and incentive compensation as defined in this regulation, aggregatingfive
hundred dollars ($500) within 12 months prior to the time when the
relevant governmental decision is made. A public official's income
includes income which has been promised to the public official but not yet
received by him or her, if he or she has alegally enforceable right to the
promised income."
Regulation 1 8703.3 (c)( l ) provides that "' [c]ommission income' means gross
payments received by a public official as a result of services rendered as a broker, agent,
or other salesperson for a specific sale or similar transaction. Commission income is
received when it is paid or credited."

Councilmember Ross is a licensed real estate broker, any commission income he receives
from his source of income as set forth in your facts will be for his role as areal estate
sales agent or other salesperson.
Regulation 18703.3(c)Q)(C) provides that sources of commission income in a
specific sale or similar transaction include for a real estate agent:
"(i) The broker and broker business entity under whose auspices
the agent works;
(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and
(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for
referring aparty to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker."
Regulation 18703.3(c)(2XDXiii) provides that sources of commission income in a
specific sale or similar transaction for a salesperson include the "business entity for
which the agent or salesperson is arr agent."
Under the facts you have presented, assuming Councilmember Ross receives
income aggregating $500 or more in the twelve-month period prior to the time the
govefirmental decision is made, Councilmernber Ross has a source of income economic
interest in his employer RCRPM for his salary and the commission income derived from
the leases he facilitates. As long as Councilmember Ross does not represent any other
person in the lease transaction, other than RCRPM, he would have no other source of
income interest in the commission he receives for facilitating lease agreements for
RCRPM as described in your facts.
Additionally, Councilmember Ross has an economic interest in RCR, assuming
he receives more than $500 in the twelve-month period prior to the governmental
decision, for commission income he receives as a sales agent for the sale and purchase of
real property. Councilmember Ross will also have an economic interest in anyperson he
represents in the transaction; again, assuming he receives a commission of $500 or more.
You have also indicated that Councilmember Ross has his own real estate
broker's license. If Councilmember Ross receives income of $500 from any source for
sale of real property, uqder his real estate broker's license, he would also have an
economic interest in the source of that income. (See regulation 18703.3(c)(Z)(B).)a },ou
have not given us any facts with respect to other potential sources of income economic
interests.
Property in Escrow: We have consistently advised that merely having a listing on
a property does not constitute promised income. (Randolph Advice Letter, No. I-02-
304.) However, once Councilmember Ross secures a purchaser and"asale is pending on
the listed property, the client is deemed to be a source ofpromised income. Once escrow
is opened, there is a buyer and a seller, and the element of speculation has diminished."
(Randolph, supra; Hahn Advice Letter, No. 4-99-239.) "Therefore, those who have
entered into an escrow agreement or acfually paid commission income during the past
twelve months are considered to be sources of income." (Randolph, supra; Wittier
Advice Letter, No. 4-00-164.)
Once the escrow has closed, and the title has been transferred, Councilmember
Ross would still have an economic interest in his source of income for the commission
received on the sale of the property. Whether or not this would present a conflict of
interest for Councilmernber Ross in participating in a governmental decision depends on
the degree of involvement and the corresponding material affects of the governmental
decision on that economic interest.
Since you have not provided information with respect to other potential economic
interests, such as the clients Councilmember Ross represents in any real estate
transactions,for purposes of this analysis we consider only Councilmember Ross's
economic interest tn RCRPM and RCR-
Srpp 4: Is Tm Ecowonznc Ixrrnpsr Drnncrr,y oR INDTRECTLy Ixvor,vro rN TEE
GovnnNyrENTAL Drcrsron ?
In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions that affect
a This would include any person he represents, any person represented by an agent working under
his brokerage license, any brokerage entity through which he conducts his business, and any person to
whom he-pays a finders fee for referring a party to the transaction to him.
5 Councilmember Ross is urged to consider other possible sources of income that may give rise to
an economic interest prohibiting him from participating in the governmental decision. For example, if he
secures a buyer for the property he has listed within the Specific Plan area, this may give rise to a conflict
of interest prohibiting him from participating in the govemmental decision as a result of the economic
interest he has in that source of income.
sonrces of income, the standards set forth in regulation 18704.1 aWly. (Regulation
rc70a@)Q).)
Regulation 187 }a.l @) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agerLcy when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeaT, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's ageflcy.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
Under the facts you have presented, it appears that both RCRPM and RCR are
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision.
Srrp 5: MlrrnraurySrANDARD
Source of fncome: Once the degree of involvement is determined, Step 5 of the
conflict of interest analysis addresses the applicable materiality standard. Regulation
18705.3(b) sets forth the materiality standards for economic interests in a source of income
that are indirectly involved in the governmdntal decision. Under regulation 18705.3(bxl),
for sources of income that are business entities, the materiality standards set forth in
regulation 18705.1(c) apply. Underregulation 13705.1(c), the applicablemateriality
standards are determined by the size of the business. For example, under regulation
18705.1(c)(4), the financial effect of a governmental decision on a relatively small business
that is indirectly involved in the govemmental decision is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decjsion will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business antity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,
(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Srnp 6: RrasoNasr.y Fonnsrnanr,p
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulationl8706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certainto be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Regulation 18706(b) and (c) provide:
"(b) In determining whether a governmental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on an economic
interest as defined in subdivision (a) above, the following factors
should be considered. These factors are not intended to be an
exclusive list of the relevant facts that may be considered in
determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable,
but are included as general guidelines:
"(1) The extent to which the official or the official,s source
of income has engaged, is engaged, orplans on engaging in
business activity in the jurisdiction;
"Q) The market share held by the official or the official,s
source of income in the jurisdiction;
"(3) The extent to which the official or the official,s source
of income has competition for business in the jurisdiction;
"(4) The scope of the govemmental decision in question;
"(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material
financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not
including future govenrmental decisions by the official,s'agency,
or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the official's agency.
and


File No. I-O5-021
"(c) Possession ofa real estate sales or brokerage license,
or any other professional license, without regard to the official,s
business activity or likely business activity, does not in itself make
a material frtancial effect on the official's economic interests
reasonably foreseeable. "
The question is essentially whether the approval of the Specific Plan will so
significantly increase business opportunities for RCRPM and RCR that the increased
opportunities constitute a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect. (Olson Advice
Letter, No. 4-00-237 copy enclosed; Bardos Advice Letter,No. A-99-l 99; rhorner,
supra.)
For example, in the Teasley Advice Letter, No. I-97-545,we advised:
"Your questions indicate that you and other agents of
Coldwell Banker Town & County intend to take advxiage of the
business opportunities that will arise as a result of the development
at Hidden Creek Ranch (e.g., becoming a listing orbuyers, agent
when homes or commercial development built under the specific
plan are put on the resale market; becoming a listing agent for
home owners who want to sell their existing homes in order to
purchase new homes built under the specific plan; becoming a
referring source for resale listings for one or more merchant
builders; opening a New Home Sales Division; selling parcels
owned by the seven separate owners). Therefore, it is reasonably
foreseeable that approval of the specific plan will have a financial
effect upon you and Coldwell Banker Town & Country, a
potentially disqualifying source of income to you....,,
However, you have not provided any facts relative to the specific governmental
decision in question that would enable us to evaluate its potential financial effects on
Councilmember Ross's economic interest as outlined above. (See Olson, supra and
Teasley, supra.) Unless the financial effects of the govemmental decision are
substantially likely to cause a material financial effect under regulation 18705.1(c) on one
of Councilmember Ross's economic interests, he would not have a conflict of interest in
participating in the decision. The mere fact that RCRPM and RCR either manage or list
for sale, and lease property within the specific plan area is not sufficient to establish the
reasonably foreseeability of a material financial effect on Councilmember Ross's
economic interests as a result of the governmental decision.
srnps 7 ANo 8: Purr,rc GrNnnar.lv & Lrcnr,r,v Rrqurnrn p.tRrrcrpATroN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally', or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable herein.

QUESTION: Does Councilmember Ross have a potential conflict of interest under the Act that
would prohibit him from participating in a govemmental decision involving the specific
plan where:
(a). The business from which he receives a source of income manages properties
that are located in the specific plan area;
(b). He has received commissions from this source of income for obtaining
tenants for leases on properties located within the specific plan area;
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2 Informal assistance does not "o.rf". 
the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
(c). He is a licensed real estate broker and has obtained a listing, as a real estate
agent and independent contractor, for another one of his sources of income, on property
located within the specific plan area;
(d). Each of these sources of income have a leasehold interest in the proposed
specific plan area;
(e). He secures a purchaser for the property in (c), above, for which he would be
entitled to receive a commission in excess of $500, and the escrow opens, but does not
close, or if the escrow opens and does close, prior to the date of the final decision on the
proposed specific plarfl

CONCLUSION: (a).-(d). Councilmember Ross does not have a conflict of interest prohibiting him
from participating in the governmental decision unless the financial effects of the
govemmental decision are substantially likely to cause amateial financial effect on
either of Councilmember Ross's source of income economic interests in (a) through (d)
above, and as further discussed below.
(e). Once Councilmember Ross has secured a purchaser, and an escrow has
opened, he has an economic interest in any person he represents in the transaction, as the
source of commission income' he is entitled to receive, as well as the business entity
under whose auspices he works.
